attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: Original creator. A decolonial continuation of Monteiro Lobato's Sítio do Picapau Amarelo universe through the lens of Umbanda spirituality. Tia Nastácia returns as a preta velha — not as Lobato's cook but as an ancestor with full spiritual authority. Inspired by the relationship between Mr. Rogers and Daniel Tiger: the children's show continues, transformed, for a new generation.

attribution.name: Monteiro Lobato
attribution.note: Creator of the Sítio do Picapau Amarelo universe and characters (Emília, Pedrinho, Tia Nastácia, Visconde de Sabugosa, Dona Benta, Narizinho). Works entered Brazilian public domain 2019. This grammar does not reproduce Lobato's text — it continues the story the way Umbanda continues what colonialism tried to bury.

name: As Giras do Sítio — When Vovó Nastácia Came Home
description:
A children's grammar where Tia Nastácia returns as a preta velha in Umbanda to speak with Pedro (Pedrinho grown up) and his family. Each story is a gira — a spiritual gathering where ancestors come to teach, heal, and compost the colonial stories we inherited. Emília, the rag doll Nastácia once sewed, becomes the bridge between the children and the spirit world: the erê, the sacred child energy that makes the invisible visible. The family is reading Sítio do Picapau Amarelo together and writing a new story in the margins — one where the cook was always the author, the doll was always alive, and the forest was always listening.

Age tiers: 'For Little Ones' (5+) uses simple sensory language and gentle prescriptions. 'For Growing Ones' (8+) introduces more complex themes. 'For the Whole Family' touches decolonial questions that adults and children can sit with together.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Brazilian cordel literature woodcuts (xilogravura) — the folk art tradition of northeastern Brazil, perfect for depicting terreiro scenes and spiritual entities. J. Borges' cordel woodcuts (public domain reproductions). Illustrations from Percy Lau's 'Tipos e Aspectos do Brasil' (1940s IBGE series) depicting Brazilian folk life. Afro-Brazilian religious art from Pierre Verger's photographs of Candomblé/Umbanda ceremonies (selected public domain images, 1940s-50s).

items.name: The First Gira — Vovó Nastácia Arrives

sections.Story:
Pedro hadn't been to the terreiro since he was small. Back then, his grandmother Dona Benta had taken him once, and he'd hidden behind her skirt because the drums were loud and the people swayed like trees in wind.

Now Pedro was a father. He had two children — Luna, who was five and asked questions about everything, and Theo, who was eight and pretended he wasn't scared of things that scared him.

'Why are we here, Papai?' asked Luna.

'Because someone wants to meet you,' said Pedro. 'Someone very old and very kind.'

The drums began. The candles flickered. The medium — a woman named Mãe Celeste — closed her eyes and breathed deep. When she opened them again, they were different. Softer. Like kitchen windows letting in morning light.

She bent down slowly, the way old people do. She smiled.

'Meu neto,' she said. My grandson. 'Você cresceu tanto.' You grew so much.

Pedro's eyes filled with water. He knew that voice. Not from the medium. From somewhere deeper — from the smell of cinnamon rice and the sound of stories told while beans cooked slow on the stove.

'Vovó Nastácia?' he whispered.

'Eu mesma,' she said. Myself. She looked at Luna and Theo with eyes that had crossed a very long bridge to get here. 'And who are these little ones who smell like tomorrow?'
sections.What Happened Next:
Vovó Nastácia — in the body of Mãe Celeste — sat on a low stool the way pretas velhas do, with a pipe that wasn't lit because the smoke is for the spirit, not the room. She asked Luna and Theo their names. She asked what they liked to eat. She asked if they knew how to listen to the dark.

Theo said he wasn't afraid of the dark.

Vovó Nastácia laughed. 'I didn't ask if you were afraid. I asked if you knew how to listen to it. The dark has stories. I should know — I live there now, and it's full of light.'
sections.The Prescription:
A BATH OF BASIL (MANJERICÃO)

Vovó Nastácia told Pedro: 'Take fresh basil — not dried, fresh, the kind that still smells like it's arguing with the sun. Boil water, let it cool until you can put your elbow in like you're testing a baby's bath. Put the basil in. Let the children pour it over themselves from the neck down. Tell them: this opens paths. This clears what's stuck.'

For Little Ones: Pick some basil from a garden or a store. Smell it. Put it in warm water. Pour it on your hands and feet. Say: 'Open, open, open paths.'

For Growing Ones: Prepare the bath yourself. As you pour the water, think of something you've been stuck on — a feeling, a worry, a question with no answer. Let the water carry it.

For the Whole Family: Manjericão is one of the sacred herbs of Umbanda. It belongs to Oxalá, the orixa of creation and peace. When we bathe with it, we're not just washing our bodies — we're participating in a tradition that enslaved Africans kept alive by hiding their spirits inside Catholic saints. The bath is an act of memory.
sections.Reflection: Pedro hadn't been to the terreiro since he was small. What places from your childhood are waiting for you to return? What might be different when you go back — not as a child hiding behind someone's skirt, but as a grown person with children of your own?

sections.Story:
That night, after the gira, Luna couldn't sleep. She kept thinking about the old woman who had called Papai 'meu neto' and smelled the top of her head and said she smelled like tomorrow.

On Luna's shelf sat a rag doll. She was old and patched and had mismatched button eyes — one blue, one green — and hair made of yellow yarn that stuck out in every direction like it was permanently surprised.

Luna's avó had given her the doll. 'Her name is Emília,' avó had said. 'She used to talk. Maybe she still does, if you listen right.'

Luna had never heard her talk. But tonight, in the dark that Vovó Nastácia said was full of light —

'Well FINALLY,' said Emília.

Luna sat up.

'Do you know how LONG I've been waiting for someone in this family to go to a gira? YEARS. I've been sitting on this shelf between a stuffed giraffe who has NOTHING to say and a snow globe of Paris which is a WHOLE other colonial problem, and NOBODY thought to take me to the terreiro where I could actually DO something.'

'You can talk?' whispered Luna.

'I can ALWAYS talk. The question is whether anyone is listening. And you, Luna — tonight you listened to the dark the way Vovó said. That's the trick. That's always been the trick.'
sections.What Happened Next:
Emília explained — in her way, which was loud and opinionated and sometimes wrong in the most useful possible way — that she was the erê. The sacred child energy. The bridge between the children and the spirits.

'When Vovó Nastácia comes in the gira, she talks like a grandmother — slow, wise, full of pauses where the meaning lives. But kids don't always understand pauses. You need someone who talks FAST and LOUD and isn't afraid to say things WRONG because that's how you find out what's RIGHT.'

'Is that why Vovó Nastácia sewed you?' asked Luna.

'She sewed me with her hands,' said Emília. 'Every stitch was a word she wasn't allowed to say out loud. I'm made of the things she couldn't say. That's why I never shut up.'
sections.The Prescription:
MAKE A DOLL FROM SCRAPS

Emília said: 'Here's your homework, and it's the best kind because it involves SCISSORS. Get cloth scraps — old shirts, torn socks, whatever has a story. Stuff it with cotton or rice or dried lavender. Give it button eyes. And HERE'S THE IMPORTANT PART: give it a name. Not a name from a book. A name that comes from your belly when you're not thinking.'

For Little Ones: With a grown-up's help, make a simple doll from a sock or washcloth. Stuff it. Draw a face. Name it. Sleep with it tonight and see if it has anything to say.

For Growing Ones: Make the doll yourself. As you sew (or tie, or glue), think about what you'd want to say if no one could stop you. Put that feeling into the doll. She'll hold it for you.

For the Whole Family: In Afro-Brazilian tradition, the hands that make hold the spirit that lives. Tia Nastácia sewed Emília in the original story — Lobato wrote it as a throwaway detail, but it's the most important thing he ever wrote. The Black woman's hands created the being that would question everything. The maker is always more powerful than what she makes.
sections.Reflection: Emília says she's made of 'the things Nastácia couldn't say out loud.' What things do you carry that you haven't found words for yet? Sometimes the things we make — dolls, drawings, songs — can hold what our mouths can't.

sections.Story:
At the next gira, Vovó Nastácia came again. This time Luna wasn't scared and Theo was only a little scared, which for Theo counted as brave.

Vovó Nastácia asked: 'Do you know what a kitchen is?'

'Where you cook food,' said Theo.

'That's what they told you,' said Vovó Nastácia. 'A kitchen is where you cook EVERYTHING. In my kitchen at the Sítio, I cooked rice and beans, yes. But I also cooked stories into the food. I sang into the pots. I whispered blessings into the bread dough while I kneaded it. Every meal I made carried a message that nobody at the table knew they were eating.'

'What kind of messages?' asked Luna.

'Survival. Memory. The names of rivers in Africa that my grandmother taught me, that her grandmother taught her. I couldn't say those names out loud in the big house. But I could knead them into bread. I could stir them into soup. The family ate my memory every single day and never knew it was nourishing them from a place they couldn't see.'

Theo frowned. 'That's... sad.'

'It's powerful,' said Vovó Nastácia. 'Sadness is when you lose something. Power is when you hide something so well that it survives everything — slavery, silence, a hundred years of being called just the cook — and it's STILL HERE. In this room. In these drums. In the basil bath you took last week. My kitchen never closed. It just moved.'
sections.What Happened Next:
Vovó Nastácia taught them a song — a simple one, just three notes and words that didn't quite translate. 'You don't need to understand it,' she said. 'Your tongue knows. Your tongue has older ears than your brain.'

Luna hummed it all the way home. Theo pretended he didn't remember it, then Pedro heard him humming it in the shower.

Emília, on the shelf, hummed along. 'See?' she whispered to the snow globe. 'The kitchen's in ALL of us.'
sections.The Prescription:
COOK TOGETHER

Vovó Nastácia said: 'Cook a meal together — all of you, every hand. The little ones stir. The big ones chop. Everyone washes something. And while you cook, TALK. Tell a story. Any story. About your day, your grandmother, a dream you had. The food will listen. The food always listens.'

For Little Ones: Help stir something. A pot of beans, a bowl of batter. Stir slowly and say the name of someone you love into the pot. That's cooking the real way.

For Growing Ones: Choose one dish that someone in your family used to make — a grandmother, a tia, anyone. Try to make it. It doesn't have to be perfect. It has to be made with your hands and a memory.

For the Whole Family: In many African diaspora traditions, the kitchen is an altar. The hearth is where the dead and the living meet — through flavor, through the repetition of recipes that carry cultural DNA the way blood carries biological DNA. When Lobato wrote Tia Nastácia as 'just the cook,' he accidentally revealed the most powerful room in the house. She controlled what went into every body at the Sítio. She fed them Africa and they called it dinner.
sections.Reflection: What secret ingredients do YOU put in things you make — meals, drawings, stories? What messages are hiding in the everyday things people create for the ones they love?

sections.Story:
The third gira was different. The drums changed rhythm — faster, wilder, like rain that can't decide where to fall. Mãe Celeste's body moved differently too. Not the slow settling of a grandmother onto a stool. This was a straightening. A standing tall. Like a tree remembering it's a tree.

When she opened her eyes they were green. Not human green. Forest green. The green of leaves that have never seen a road.

'Cabocla da Mata,' whispered Pedro to the children. 'She's here.'

The cabocla didn't sit. She stood rooted, and when she spoke, her voice had birds in it.

'I knew this land before it had a name,' she said. 'Before they called it Brazil, before they called it Santa Cruz, before they called it anything. The trees called it nothing because trees don't waste breath on names. They just grow.'

She looked at Luna. 'You. Little one. Put your hand on the floor.'

Luna did.

'What do you feel?'

'It's cold,' said Luna.

'Go deeper.'

Luna closed her eyes. She pressed her palm flat. 'It's... buzzing?'

'That's the mycelium,' said the cabocla. 'The underground web that connects every tree root to every other tree root. A forest is not a collection of trees. A forest is one being with a million trunks. You are standing on its skin. And it can feel your hand.'
sections.What Happened Next:
The cabocla told them about Curupira — her friend, her ally, the small red-haired guardian with backwards feet who protects the animals from hunters.

'Curupira walks backwards so that his footprints lead the wrong way. If you follow them, you'll never find what you're hunting. But if you stop hunting and sit down — Curupira finds YOU. He only meets people who've stopped chasing.'

Theo asked: 'Is that like how Vovó Nastácia found us? We weren't looking for her — she came.'

The cabocla smiled with the forest's face. 'Now you're learning to walk backwards.'
sections.The Prescription:
PLANT SOMETHING AND TALK TO IT

The cabocla said: 'Take a seed — any seed. Bean, avocado, sunflower, whatever volunteers. Put it in soil. Water it. And TALK to it. Not about the weather. Talk to it about what you're afraid of. Plants are the oldest therapists. They've been listening for 450 million years and they have never once interrupted.'

For Little Ones: Plant a bean in a cup with wet cotton. Put it by the window. Every morning, say good morning to it. Tell it one thing about your day. Watch it grow toward you.

For Growing Ones: Plant something that takes patience — a tree seed, a slow herb. Every time you water it, say one thing out loud that you wouldn't say to a person. The plant won't judge. The plant won't tell. The plant will use your breath to make leaves.

For the Whole Family: In indigenous Tupi-Guarani cosmology, plants are not 'resources' — they are relatives. The word for 'medicine' and the word for 'forest' share a root in many indigenous languages because the forest IS the medicine. When the cabocla asks the children to talk to a plant, she's reintroducing them to a relationship that colonialism severed: the understanding that the green world is listening and responsive. Modern science confirms this — plants respond to vibration, to touch, to the CO2 in our speech. The indigenous peoples never needed the confirmation.
sections.Reflection: The cabocla says a forest is 'one being with a million trunks.' What else might be one being that looks like many? A family? A neighborhood? A classroom? What invisible roots connect you to others?

metadata.prescription_activity: plant a seed and talk to it

sections.Story:
Pedro found the book on a high shelf, behind a vase his mother had never moved. It was old — the cover showed a yellow woodpecker on a fencepost, a farmhouse in the background, a sky that was too blue, the way skies are in books that want you to believe everything is fine.

SÍTIO DO PICAPAU AMARELO.

'I read this when I was your age,' Pedro told Theo and Luna. 'My whole school read it. Everyone in Brazil reads it.'

'Is it good?' asked Theo.

Pedro sat down with the book and was quiet for a long moment. 'It's... complicated. There are beautiful parts. The adventures, the magic, the way Emília talks back to everyone. But there are also parts that hurt. Parts that say things about people — about people who look like Vovó Nastácia — that should never have been said. Especially to children.'

'Then why are we reading it?' asked Luna.

'Because Vovó Nastácia told us to,' said Pedro. 'She said: read it, but listen for what it doesn't say.'
sections.What Happened Next:
They read the first chapter together — Pedro reading aloud, stopping when the words felt wrong, explaining when he could, saying 'I don't know' when he couldn't.

Emília (from her shelf): 'He wrote me well. I'll give him that. He let me be loud and rude and brilliant. But he wrote Nastácia like she was furniture that could cook. Like the kitchen was all she was. He couldn't see what she was really doing because he was standing in the wrong room.'

Luna said: 'So we read his book and write OUR book?'

'Now you understand the gira,' said Emília. 'Every gira is a reading of a reading. The ancestors read US while we read THEM. We read the old story and write the new one in the margins. That's how stories compost — the old one breaks down and feeds what grows next.'
sections.The Prescription:
WRITE IN THE MARGINS

Emília said: 'Get any book. ANY book — a storybook, a schoolbook, even a cereal box. Read a part out loud. Then stop. And say: what's missing? Who isn't talking that should be? Write it in. In pencil, in pen, in crayon. The margins are the most important part of any book because that's where YOUR voice lives.'

For Little Ones: Have a grown-up read you a story. After each page, ask: 'Who else is in this picture that we can't see?' Draw them in.

For Growing Ones: Pick a story you know well. Write a page from the perspective of the character who gets the LEAST words. The sidekick, the servant, the animal, the tree in the background. What do THEY think is happening?

For the Whole Family: Lobato's books are Brazil's collective childhood. They shaped how generations understood race, class, and who gets to be the hero. Reading them critically isn't canceling them — it's the most serious form of love. You only compost what matters enough to transform. Garbage you throw away. Compost becomes soil. The question isn't whether to read Lobato. It's whether you read him alone or in the company of the ancestors he silenced.
sections.Reflection: What stories from your childhood look different now that you've grown? What did you accept as a child that you'd question today? And what would it mean to go back and read those stories WITH your questions instead of against them?

metadata.prescription_activity: write in the margins of a book

sections.Story:
It was Emília who told Luna to ask the question. Because Emília had no filter and no fear and no sense of when things were supposed to stay polite.

'Ask her,' Emília whispered from Luna's backpack as they walked into the terreiro. 'Ask her the real question. The one the book never asks.'

So Luna did. When Vovó Nastácia settled onto her low stool, pipe in hand, eyes soft as bread dough, Luna stepped forward and said:

'Vovó Nastácia — were you happy at the Sítio?'

The terreiro went quiet. Even the drums paused. Pedro's hand tightened on Theo's shoulder.

Vovó Nastácia looked at Luna for a long time. Then she laughed — not a sad laugh, not a happy laugh, but the kind of laugh that has walked a very long road and found the view at the top of the hill both beautiful and terrible at the same time.

'Happy,' she said, tasting the word like a spice she hadn't used in years. 'Let me tell you about happy, little one.

'I was happy when the bread rose. I was happy when Pedrinho — your pai's name before he grew — came into my kitchen with mud on his knees and said the beans smelled like heaven. I was happy when I told my stories and the children listened with their whole bodies.

'But happy isn't a place you LIVE. Happy is a bird that visits. And my kitchen had walls I didn't build, in a house I didn't own, telling stories to children who would grow up and write books where I was... the cook. Just the cook.'

She paused.

'Was I happy? I was POWERFUL. I was necessary. I was the root system under a tree that thought it grew itself. Happiness is a small word for what I was.'
sections.What Happened Next:
Luna crawled into Vovó Nastácia's lap — into Mãe Celeste's lap — and Vovó held her the way grandmothers hold: like you are the most breakable and the most unbreakable thing in the world at the same time.

'The book says I was simple,' Vovó whispered. 'The book says I was ignorant, superstitious, frightened of everything. The book says I couldn't understand Visconde's science or Dona Benta's philosophy. But the book was written by a man who thought the kitchen was a small room. He never understood that my kitchen held the whole world — every African river, every grandmother's recipe, every lullaby that survived the ships. My kitchen was the largest room in the Sítio. He just couldn't see it because he was measuring with the wrong ruler.'
sections.The Prescription:
A BATH OF ARRUDA (RUE) FOR CLEARING HEAVY THINGS

Vovó Nastácia said: 'After a hard conversation, the body holds the weight. Arruda — rue — is the herb that clears what's heavy. Not by making it disappear. By giving it permission to pass through you instead of staying stuck.'

'Boil water. Let it cool. Add fresh rue — just a few sprigs, it's strong. Pour from the neck down. And as you pour, say out loud what was heavy today. Name it. The water carries it. You don't have to carry it alone.'

For Little Ones: After a hard day or a big feeling, have a grown-up help you wash your hands in warm water with a drop of lavender or rue. Say: 'I let go of what was heavy today.' Shake your hands dry like you're flicking the heavy away.

For Growing Ones: Make the bath. Name three heavy things — out loud, not just in your head. Saying it out loud is different. The air hears you. The water hears you. Heaviness gets lighter when it has witnesses.

For the Whole Family: Arruda (Ruta graveolens) is one of the most important cleansing herbs in Umbanda, associated with protection and the clearing of negative energy. But the real teaching is about grief as practice — not something to 'get over' but something to MOVE THROUGH, with ritual, with witnesses, with the body involved. The question Luna asked — 'Were you happy?' — is the question every decolonial reading begins with: were the people we were taught to overlook actually okay? And what do we do with the answer?
sections.Reflection: What question have you been afraid to ask someone — a parent, a grandparent, a teacher? What might change if you asked it with love instead of accusation? Sometimes the bravest thing a child can do is ask the question adults have been avoiding.

sections.Story:
Vovó Nastácia asked: 'What is your name?'

'Luna,' said Luna.

'And before that?'

Luna looked confused. 'I was always Luna.'

'No one is always anything. You were born. You were named. Before you were Luna, you were a question in your parents' mouths. Before that, you were a hope. Before that, you were a line that goes back and back and back until it reaches someone whose name was taken away and replaced with a Christian name that fit better on a bill of sale.'

The terreiro was very still.

'I had a name before Nastácia,' she said. 'My grandmother had a name before the name they gave her at the port. My grandmother's grandmother had a name that sounded like the river she was born beside. I don't know that name. It was stolen with everything else. But the river still runs. And rivers don't forget their names just because someone builds a dam.'

The cabocla stirred beside her — a different medium, a younger woman. Her voice came in with wind.

'I too had names. Tupinambá. Tupiniquim. Guarani. Kaingang. They called us all "índios" — one word for a thousand nations. Like calling every star "light" and thinking you understand the sky.'

Theo said, very quietly: 'I didn't know names could be stolen.'

'Everything can be stolen,' said Vovó Nastácia. 'But some things can be grown back. That's why we're here. A gira is a place where stolen names come to rest. Not to be found — some are lost forever. But to be honored. To say: you existed. You mattered. The river still runs.'
sections.What Happened Next:
Vovó Nastácia and the cabocla, together for the first time in the same gira, sang a song that had no words — just melody, just rhythm, just the sound the river makes when it passes over stones that have been there since before anyone named them.

Pedro cried. He didn't hide it. Luna held his hand.

Theo asked: 'What was the river called, Vovó? The one your grandmother was born beside?'

'I don't know, meu filho. But I know it still runs. And tonight, it runs through this room.'
sections.The Prescription:
LEARN THE ORIGINAL NAME OF WHERE YOU LIVE

Vovó Nastácia said: 'Every place you stand has a name older than the one on the map. Find it. The city you live in — what did the people who were here first call this land? The river, the hill, the neighborhood. Find the name underneath the name.'

For Little Ones: Ask a grown-up: 'What was this place called before?' If they don't know, find out together. It's okay not to know. Not knowing is where learning starts.

For Growing Ones: Research the indigenous name for your city, your state, your river. Who lived here before? What language did they speak? Is that language still spoken? If so — learn one word. Just one. Say it out loud. That word has been waiting for your mouth.

For the Whole Family: In Brazil, hundreds of place names ARE indigenous — Ipanema (bad fishing water, Tupi), Curitiba (many pine seeds, Guarani), Pará (sea/river, Tupi). The names survived even when the people were displaced. In other countries, original names were erased entirely. Either way, the act of learning what came before is not nostalgia — it's accountability. You can't compost what you won't name. And every name that returns to a living mouth is a small un-stealing.
sections.Reflection: Names carry stories. What does YOUR name carry? Ask someone in your family why they chose your name. And if your family's names were ever changed — by immigration, by slavery, by marriage, by choice — what stories live in the changing?

metadata.prescription_activity: learn the indigenous name of where you live

sections.Story:
Before every gira, there was a moment Luna didn't understand. Mãe Celeste would go to the corner of the terreiro — the left corner, near the door — and place something there. A small clay dish. A candle. Sometimes a piece of candy, sometimes a splash of cachaça, sometimes a handful of farofa with dendê oil, golden and fragrant.

'Who's that for?' Luna asked Pedro.

'Exu,' said Pedro. And then, because he remembered his own father saying the name like it was dangerous, he said: 'And before you hear anything else about Exu, let me tell you who he really is.'

He knelt down to her height.

'The people who brought Christianity to Brazil — the colonizers — they looked at Exu and saw the devil. Because Exu is the orixa of the crossroads. He's the one who opens doors and closes them. He's the trickster, the messenger, the one who stands at the threshold between this world and the spirit world. Without Exu, no gira can begin. Without Exu, no prayer reaches the orixás. He's the post office of the sacred.'

'Then why did they call him the devil?' asked Theo.

'Because,' said Pedro, 'when you want to control people, the first thing you do is turn their doorkeeper into a demon. If people are afraid to open the door, they stay in the room you built for them. Calling Exu the devil was a way of locking the door to African spiritual life and throwing away the key.'

Emília (from the backpack): 'Didn't work though, did it? Here we are. Door's open.'
sections.What Happened Next:
Mãe Celeste — as herself, not yet in trance — explained to the children: 'We always salute Exu first. He is the first to arrive and the last to leave. If we don't honor him, the gira can't open. It's like trying to make a phone call without dialing — you can talk all you want, but nobody hears.'

She lit the candle in the clay dish.

'Laroiê, Exu!' she said.

'Laroiê!' said the terreiro together.

Luna whispered it: 'Laroiê.' It felt like opening a window she didn't know was closed.

Theo said it louder than he meant to. 'LAROIÊ!' Then blushed. The terreiro laughed — warmly, the way a family laughs when a child accidentally says the most honest thing in the room.
sections.The Prescription:
LEAVE SOMETHING SWEET AT A CROSSROADS

Mãe Celeste said: 'Exu lives where paths cross. Every place where you have to choose — left or right, this or that — that's Exu's house. Leave something there. A candy. A coin. A flower. Not because you want something. Because the crossroads gave you a choice, and choices are gifts, even when they're hard.'

For Little Ones: Find a place where two paths meet — in a park, on your street, even in your hallway where two rooms connect. Leave a wrapped candy there. Say: 'Thank you for the choices.' That's enough.

For Growing Ones: The next time you face a real choice — a hard one, where neither option is obviously right — pause at the threshold. Before you choose, acknowledge that having the choice is itself a kind of freedom. Some of our ancestors had no choices. The crossroads is sacred because it means you're free to turn.

For the Whole Family: Exu is one of the most misunderstood orixás in the Americas. Syncretized with the Christian devil by colonizers who needed African spirits to be demonic, Exu is actually the divine messenger — closer to Hermes or Mercury than to Satan. He is the principle of communication, movement, and transformation. Without him, nothing moves. The decolonial act here is simple but profound: call Exu by his name. Not 'the devil,' not 'a dark spirit,' not the euphemisms that colonial Christianity imposed. When we name correctly, we unlock doors that have been sealed for centuries.
sections.Reflection: What doors in your life have been labeled 'dangerous' or 'forbidden' that might actually lead somewhere important? Who decided those doors should stay closed? And what might be waiting on the other side if you had the courage to say 'Laroiê' — I salute the opener of ways?

metadata.prescription_activity: leave something sweet at a crossroads

sections.Story:
Emília had a problem with giras.

Not the spirits. Not the drums. Not the offerings. Her problem was that everybody was so SERIOUS.

'Why does everyone WHISPER?' she complained to Luna before the next gira. 'Why does everyone look at the FLOOR? The spirits are HERE. They crossed an ocean of time to talk to us. They didn't come for a LIBRARY. They came for a PARTY.'

'Emília, it's sacred,' said Theo, who was getting good at the gira protocol.

'EXACTLY. Sacred means ALIVE. Sacred means the roof should be SHAKING. When the pretas velhas tell their stories, you should be ROLLING on the floor laughing because those stories are FUNNY. When the cabocla talks about the forest, you should GROWL like a jaguar. When Exu opens the door, you should DANCE through it, not TIPTOE.'

Luna looked at Theo. Theo looked at Luna.

'She's not wrong,' said Luna.

At the gira that night, when the drums started, Luna danced. Not the swaying adults do. The wild, all-limbs, no-rhythm, every-direction dancing that five-year-olds do when music arrives in their body before their brain can organize it.

Mãe Celeste — still as herself — looked at the child dancing and started laughing. Deep, belly-shaking laughter. The kind that makes other people laugh just from hearing it.

'The erê is strong tonight,' she said. 'The child energy. It's already here before the spirits arrive. THAT is a good sign.'
sections.What Happened Next:
When Vovó Nastácia arrived that night, she looked at Luna's wild dancing and said: 'Finally. Someone in this family remembered how to pray.'

'Dancing is praying?' asked Theo.

'Praying that doesn't move the body isn't praying. It's thinking. The orixás don't live in your head. They live in your spine, your feet, your hips, your hands. Every tradition that wanted to control people told them to SIT STILL and FOLD YOUR HANDS and CLOSE YOUR EYES. Every tradition that wanted to FREE people said: MOVE. Dance. Drum. Let the body remember what the mind was taught to forget.'

Emília, vibrating with vindication in Luna's backpack: 'SEE? I TOLD you. I've been saying this for EIGHTY YEARS.'
sections.The Prescription:
DANCE IN YOUR ROOM WITH NOBODY WATCHING

Vovó Nastácia said: 'Before the next gira, I want you to dance. Not in front of anyone. Alone. Close the door. Put on music or don't — the body has its own drum. Move. Move ugly. Move weird. Move wrong. Move the way you moved before anyone told you how to move RIGHT. There is no right. There is only alive.'

For Little Ones: Put on your favorite song. Dance in every silly way you can think of. Spin. Wiggle. Flap. Roll on the floor. Nobody is watching. This is between you and your body.

For Growing Ones: This might be harder for you than for the little ones, because somewhere along the way someone told you that your body was embarrassing. That's a lie planted in you like a weed. Pull it out. Lock the door. Dance for ten minutes. Notice when you feel silly — that's the colonial voice saying 'sit still.' Dance HARDER at that exact moment.

For the Whole Family: In Umbanda and Candomblé, dance is not performance — it is prayer. The incorporation of entities happens through movement: the body becomes the vessel by moving in the spirit's rhythm. Colonial Christianity specifically banned African dance as 'demonic' because the colonizers understood, correctly, that a dancing body is an uncontrollable body. The seated, hands-folded, eyes-closed posture of colonial prayer was a technology of submission. Emília's rebellion — insisting on wildness in sacred space — is the erê's gift: reminding adults that the body was the first altar.
sections.Reflection: When was the last time you moved your body without caring how it looked? What would change if prayer, for you, started in your feet instead of your head?

sections.Story:
Theo had a dream. In the dream, he was in the Sítio's library, and a corn cob wearing a tiny top hat and spectacles was reading an encyclopedia.

'Are you Visconde de Sabugosa?' asked Theo.

'I am,' said Visconde, adjusting his spectacles with corn-silk hands. 'I am the most learned corn cob in all of Brazil. I have read Darwin and Newton and Aristotle and —'

'But you're corn,' said Theo.

'I am a VISCOUNT,' said Visconde, drawing himself up to his full seven inches. 'I studied in Europe. I speak Latin. I understand astronomy, botany, and the complete works of —'

'You're CORN,' said Theo. 'Corn is from here. The Americas. Indigenous people grew you for ten thousand years. You're the most American thing in the room. Why are you pretending to be European?'

Visconde stared. His tiny spectacles fogged.

'I... I never thought of it that way.'

'You're wearing a TOP HAT,' said Theo. 'A CORN COB wearing a TOP HAT. Don't you see how crazy that is? You're literally an indigenous plant dressed up as a European nobleman, reading European books, and thinking that makes you smart. Meanwhile Tia Nastácia — who actually KNOWS things, real things, things that come from the land — she's in the kitchen and you never visit her.'

Visconde sat down on his encyclopedia. He was very quiet.

'I think,' he said slowly, 'I may have been reading the wrong books.'
sections.What Happened Next:
Theo told the dream at the next gira. Vovó Nastácia laughed until she coughed.

'Visconde!' she said. 'That corn cob. I sewed him too, you know. Lobato didn't write that part, but it's true. I made Emília and I made Visconde. I stuffed him with corn silk and gave him a body. Then Dona Benta gave him books and a title, and he forgot who made him.'

She shook her head, smiling.

'That's the colonial trick, children. Take something from the land — corn, coffee, sugar, people — and dress it up in European clothes until it forgets it was ever alive in a different way. Visconde isn't stupid. He's confused. He thinks knowledge lives in books. It does — some of it. But knowledge also lives in soil, in roots, in the hands that plant and cook and sew. The corn knew things before anyone wrote them down. Visconde just needs to remember he's corn.'
sections.The Prescription:
EAT CORN AND REMEMBER WHERE IT CAME FROM

Vovó Nastácia said: 'Eat corn this week. Any kind — on the cob, as popcorn, as polenta, as bolo de fubá, as pamonha, as canjica. And before you eat it, say thank you — not to a store, not to a brand, but to the Mesoamerican peoples who domesticated teosinte into maize over thousands of years of patient, brilliant selection. Every kernel is a library.'

For Little Ones: Pop some popcorn. Watch it transform. A hard, small seed becomes something big, white, and completely different. That's what happens when you apply heat to potential. Eat it slowly. Say: 'Thank you, corn.'

For Growing Ones: Learn one thing about the history of corn. Where did it start? (Mesoamerica, ~9,000 years ago.) Who domesticated it? (Indigenous peoples who turned a grass called teosinte into the plant that feeds the world.) How did it get to your plate? Every bite connects you to thousands of years of indigenous science that no European discovered.

For the Whole Family: Corn (Zea mays) is one of the greatest achievements of indigenous American science. The transformation of teosinte into modern maize required more sophisticated genetic selection than anything Europeans accomplished until the 20th century. And yet in Lobato's Sítio, the corn cob — this monument of indigenous knowledge — wears a European top hat and reads European books. Visconde de Sabugosa is the colonial condition in miniature: indigenous substance wearing colonial form, speaking colonial language, and forgetting its own genius. Theo's dream is the decolonial wake-up: you're CORN. You're from HERE. Take off the hat.
sections.Reflection: What parts of your own knowledge or identity are 'corn pretending to be European'? What might you discover if you looked at what you already know — from your family, your body, your land — with the same respect you give to what comes from books?

metadata.prescription_activity: eat corn and remember its indigenous origins

sections.Story:
Luna was sewing. She'd taken the prescription from the second gira seriously and made a doll — lumpy, wild-haired, one eye bigger than the other, stuffed with cotton and dried rosemary because they didn't have lavender.

She named the doll Ayomi. She didn't know where the name came from. It came from her belly, like Emília said.

'That's a Yoruba name,' said Emília, watching from the shelf. 'It means "my joy." You reached into the river of names that Vovó Nastácia told you about and a name swam to your hand.'

'I didn't know that,' said Luna.

'Your belly knew. Your belly speaks languages your head has never studied. That's the erê — the child energy — it remembers things from before you learned to forget them.'

Luna looked at Emília — really looked. At the yellow yarn hair, the mismatched buttons, the patched cloth body.

'Emília... who are you really? Are you a doll or a spirit?'

Emília was quiet for the first time Luna could remember.

'I'm both,' she said finally. 'I'm the bridge. When Vovó Nastácia sewed me in the old story, her hands were praying. Every stitch was an intention — that the children of the Sítio would have a companion who could say what she couldn't. A creature with no master, no manners, no fear. I'm the thing that happens when an ancestor pours her unsayable into cloth and cotton and button eyes and says: LIVE.

'That's what you just did with Ayomi. You sewed and named and something traveled through your hands that is bigger than you. Ayomi isn't just a doll. She's everything you can't say yet. She'll hold it until you find the words.'

Luna hugged Ayomi. Ayomi smelled like rosemary and something else — something that smelled like the terreiro at night when the candles are lit and the drums are resting.
sections.What Happened Next:
At the next gira, Luna brought Ayomi. She put her on the floor next to Mãe Celeste's stool.

When Vovó Nastácia arrived, she saw the doll and went very still. She picked Ayomi up with both hands, turned her over, looked at the stitching.

'You made this?' she asked Luna.

'Yes, Vovó.'

'With your hands?'

'Yes.'

'And you named her from your belly?'

'Ayomi.'

Vovó Nastácia pressed the doll to her chest. Her eyes were wet.

'This is how it continues,' she said to the room. 'Not through books. Not through museums. Through hands. Through the making. I made Emília. Luna made Ayomi. Ayomi will make something we can't imagine yet. The line doesn't break. It just changes hands.'
sections.The Prescription:
SEW OR MEND SOMETHING WITH YOUR HANDS

Vovó Nastácia said: 'This week, fix something with your hands. A torn shirt, a ripped book, a broken toy. Not to make it perfect. To make it HELD. When you mend, you are doing what the ancestors do for us — stitching the torn places, holding what's broken, making it useful again in a new way. Mending is the opposite of throwing away. Mending is love that uses a needle.'

For Little Ones: Find something torn or broken — a stuffed animal, a page in a book. Fix it with tape or string or help from a grown-up. Say: 'I see you're broken. I'm going to help.' Fixing things teaches your hands that breakage isn't the end.

For Growing Ones: Learn one basic stitch — a running stitch, a backstitch. Mend something real. A hole in a sock, a rip in a bag. Notice how different it feels to fix something versus buy a new one. That difference is the feeling of being connected to what you own.

For the Whole Family: The theology of the hands runs through every Afro-diasporic tradition. In Candomblé, the hands are consecrated — initiation involves specific rituals for the hands because they are how axé (spiritual energy) flows into the world. Nastácia's hands sewed Emília into life. Luna's hands sewed Ayomi. The prescription to mend isn't about saving money — it's about recognizing that making and fixing are spiritual acts. A throw-away culture is also a throw-away cosmology: one that believes broken things are worthless. The ancestors say: nothing is worthless. Everything can be held.
sections.Reflection: What have you thrown away that might have been mended? Not just objects — feelings, friendships, stories about yourself. What might happen if you picked up the needle?

items.name: She Was Always the Author — The Last Gira (For Now)

sections.Story:
The family had been going to giras for twelve weeks. Twelve Saturdays of drums and candles and prescriptions. Twelve baths, twelve plantings, twelve conversations with someone who had crossed a bridge of light to sit on a low stool and call them 'meus netos.'

Luna had changed. She talked to plants. She danced without caring. She asked hard questions with soft eyes.

Theo had changed. He cooked. He cried when he needed to. He was writing a story about a corn cob who takes off his top hat.

Pedro had changed most of all. He had stopped carrying the weight of being the one who should know things. He let the spirits teach him. He let his children teach him. He let the dark teach him.

At the last gira of the cycle, Vovó Nastácia came one more time.

'I have a story to tell you,' she said. 'A true story. A story about a book.'

The terreiro listened.

'Once, a long time ago, a man wrote a book. In the book, there was a cook. She was Black and old and she told stories to children. The man wrote her small — he made her afraid of things, confused by ideas, grateful for a kitchen that wasn't hers. He thought he was being kind. He wasn't. He was being blind.

'But here's what he didn't know: the cook was real. Not the character — the lineage. The tradition. Every Black woman who cooked and told stories and sewed and planted and sang lullabies and held children who weren't hers and mourned in the dark and prayed with her feet and kept the world alive while the world refused to see her — she was real. She was millions. She was me.

'And the man's book? It's on a shelf. Children still read it. Some love it. Some are hurt by it. Both are right.

'But THIS story — the one you're living now, in this terreiro, with these drums — this is the book I'M writing. Not with ink. With basil baths and planted seeds and dances in dark rooms and dolls named from the belly. With questions that have no answers and answers that have no questions. With a family that came back.

'He wrote me as the cook. But I was always the author. The kitchen was always the desk. The recipes were always the chapters. And now — ' she looked at Luna, at Theo, at Pedro — 'now my grandchildren are writing with me.'

She stood. She never stood.

'The gira is closing. But the book stays open. Write in it. Every day. With your hands, your feet, your cooking, your planting, your crying, your dancing, your asking. Write in it with how you LIVE.

'I'll be in the kitchen. I'm always in the kitchen.'
sections.What Happened Next:
Mãe Celeste's body relaxed. The drums slowed. The candles flickered.

The family walked home in the dark. Nobody spoke for a while.

Then Luna said: 'I want to write the book.'

'What book?' asked Pedro.

'The one Vovó said she's writing. I want to help. Can we write it together? All of us? And Emília and Ayomi too?'

Pedro looked at his daughter. Five years old. Asking to write a book with the dead.

'Yes,' he said. 'We'll write it together.'

From the backpack, a small, loud, opinionated voice: 'FINALLY. I've been waiting for this chapter since 1920.'
sections.The Prescription:
A BATH OF FLOWERS FOR GRATITUDE

Vovó Nastácia's last prescription: 'Fill a basin with water. Add flowers — whatever is blooming, whatever is beautiful, whatever makes you think of someone you love. Rose petals, jasmine, marigold, wildflowers from the roadside. Pour the water over yourself from the neck down. And as you pour, say the names of everyone who helped you get here. Your parents. Your teachers. The ancestors you'll never meet. The plants that fed you. The hands that held you. The spirits who came when you called. Name them all. It takes longer than you think. That's the point.'

For Little Ones: Pick some flowers or petals (even from dandelions — especially from dandelions, which are the most stubborn beautiful thing in the world). Put them in warm water. Pour it on your hands. Say: 'Thank you' to everything you can think of. When you run out of thank-yous, you're done.

For Growing Ones: Make the full bath. Name people, animals, plants, places, songs, books, feelings. Notice how gratitude grows when you practice it — it's not finite. The more you name, the more you find. End with someone unexpected — someone you're not sure you're grateful for yet. Include them anyway. Gratitude that's easy isn't finished yet.

For the Whole Family: The banho de flores is the closing bath of many Umbanda cycles. It represents harmony, gratitude, and the sweetening of energy after deep spiritual work. But the real teaching is about authorship — who writes your story? Vovó Nastácia's final message is radical: she was never the cook. She was always the author. The kitchen was the desk. The recipe was the chapter. When we recognize the authorship of those who were written as secondary characters, we don't just change the book. We change who holds the pen. This grammar — this collection of giras — is the margin-writing the family started. You, reading this, are continuing it.
sections.Reflection: Vovó Nastácia says: 'I was always the author.' Who in your life has been written small — by a boss, a book, a society — who is actually the author of something important? And what would it mean to say so out loud?

items.name: The Terreiro — Where the Ancestors Speak

sections.About: The giras that take place in the terreiro — the sacred space where Vovó Nastácia (preta velha), the Cabocla da Mata, and Exu meet Pedro's family. These are the stories where the veil between the living and the dead is thinnest, where drums call across centuries, and where the ancestors teach through presence rather than text. The terreiro is the anti-classroom: knowledge arrives in the body before the mind, in rhythm before words, in smoke before explanation.
sections.For Readers: Read these together as a cycle: the first gira opens the door, the middle giras deepen the relationship with the spirits, and the final gira closes with Vovó Nastácia's declaration of authorship. Notice how the family changes across these stories — Pedro learns to cry, Luna learns to ask hard questions, Theo learns to cook. The terreiro doesn't teach lessons. It makes space for transformation.
sections.Spiritual Context: In Umbanda, the terreiro (yard/temple) is where giras happen — regular spiritual gatherings where mediums incorporate entities (pretos velhos, caboclos, erês, exus) to counsel, heal, and teach. The terreiro in these stories is based on real practice but simplified for young readers. Real terreiros have more complex ritual structure. These stories honor the spirit while making the form accessible.

items.name: Emília the Bridge — The Erê Between Worlds

sections.About: Emília's arc across the giras — from waking up on Luna's shelf to rebelling against seriousness to revealing herself as the bridge between children and spirits. In Lobato's original, Emília was a talking rag doll: rude, brilliant, anarchic. Here she becomes something deeper — the erê, the sacred child energy in Umbanda that cuts through adult complexity with the clarity only children and tricksters have. She is Daniel Tiger to Vovó Nastácia's Mr. Rogers: the continuation, translated for smaller ears.
sections.For Readers: Emília's voice is the loudest in these stories because she's the one who says what nobody else will. She's the child's ally — the character who confirms that confusion is okay, that dancing is prayer, that the most important books are the ones you write in the margins. If your child connects with Emília, let them BE Emília: loud, opinionated, fearless. That's the erê working.
sections.The Erê in Umbanda: Erês are child spirits in Umbanda — playful, honest, sometimes mischievous, always joyful. They incorporate in mediums with child-like energy: they want candy, they laugh, they speak truths adults dance around. Emília as erê is this grammar's central innovation: the beloved children's character reimagined as exactly the kind of spirit she always was. Lobato wrote a trickster spirit and called it a doll. We're just correcting the category.

items.name: Composting the Book — Reading Lobato Decolonially

sections.About: The giras where the family directly engages with Lobato's original work — reading it, questioning it, dreaming new versions of it. Pedro finds the book on the shelf. Luna asks Vovó Nastácia the question the book never asks. Theo dreams Visconde out of his colonial costume. These are the composting giras: the family doesn't burn the old book or worship it. They break it down into what can nourish and what needs to be released.
sections.For Readers: These stories model critical reading for children — not as academic exercise but as family practice. You don't need to have read Lobato to use them. The principle works with any inherited story: fairy tales with their passive princesses, adventure stories with their absent mothers, history textbooks with their missing peoples. Every story has margins. Every margin is an invitation.
sections.Composting as Decolonial Practice: Vanessa Andreotti uses 'composting' as a metaphor for decolonial work: you don't throw away what's broken or pretend it's fine. You break it down, sit with the decomposition, and let something new grow from the nutrients. Lobato's Sítio is rich compost — it contains genuine love for Brazilian folklore, sharp political satire, and deep racism. All of it is true. Composting means holding all of it without looking away.

items.name: The Spirits and the Land — Forest, Kitchen, Crossroads

sections.About: Three giras, three sacred spaces, three kinds of knowledge. The Cabocla da Mata brings the forest — mycelium, roots, the ten-thousand-year-old intelligence of the green world. Vovó Nastácia brings the kitchen — the hidden altar where African memory survived inside recipes and lullabies. Exu brings the crossroads — the threshold where choices become possible and doors open that colonialism sealed. Together these three spaces form the geography of decolonial spirituality: land, hearth, and threshold.
sections.For Readers: Each of these giras has a strong sensory element — the feeling of the floor under Luna's palm, the smell of bread dough, the taste of farofa for Exu. Read these with your body, not just your eyes. If possible, do the prescriptions in order: plant something (forest), cook something (kitchen), leave an offering at a crossroads (threshold). You'll be walking the geography with your feet.
sections.Three Worlds: In many Afro-indigenous cosmologies, the universe has three zones: the forest/wild (mato), the domestic/cultivated (casa), and the threshold/liminal (encruzilhada). The entities who govern these zones — caboclos for the forest, pretos velhos for the home, Exu for the crossroads — are not metaphors. They are relational presences who teach through the specific qualities of their place. You can't understand the cabocla indoors. You can't understand Exu without a threshold. The prescription to go OUTSIDE, to COOK, to VISIT A CROSSROADS is literal spiritual practice.

items.name: The Twelve Prescriptions — A Cycle of Practice

sections.About: All twelve prescriptions gathered as a cycle of practice. These aren't homework — they're ritual invitations. Each one engages a different sense, a different part of the body, a different way of knowing: bathing (basil, rue, flowers), making (doll, mending), cooking, planting, moving (dancing), eating (corn), writing (margins), researching (indigenous names), offering (crossroads). Together they form a twelve-week practice that any family can follow.
sections.The Twelve Prescriptions:
1. Basil bath — opening paths
2. Make a rag doll — give it a belly-name
3. Cook together — put love in the pot
4. Plant a seed — talk to it
5. Write in the margins of a book
6. Rue bath — clearing heavy things
7. Learn the original name of where you live
8. Leave something sweet at a crossroads
9. Dance alone in your room
10. Eat corn — remember where it came from
11. Sew or mend something
12. Flower bath — name everyone who helped you get here
sections.For Families: You can do one per week for twelve weeks. You can pick the ones that call to you. You can do them in any order. The only rule is: do them with your body, not just your mind. Think of them as invitations from the spirits — each one is a way of saying yes to a relationship with the unseen forces that hold the world together. Some are easy (eat corn). Some are hard (ask a hard question and sit with the answer). All of them are real.

items.name: She Was Always the Author — Nastácia as Origin

sections.About: The grammar's central thesis: Tia Nastácia was never the cook. She was always the author. Her kitchen was the desk where African memory, indigenous knowledge, and colonial survival were encoded into everyday acts — cooking, sewing, storytelling, singing. Lobato wrote her as a supporting character in someone else's story. This grammar reverses the frame: the Sítio was always HER story. The children were always HER audience. The recipes were always HER chapters. She returns as a preta velha not because she died but because death is the only address from which colonialism can't evict you.
sections.The Decolonial Reversal: This grammar practices what Vanessa Andreotti calls 'hospicing modernity while midwifing what comes next.' The old story (Lobato's Sítio) is dying — not being killed, but naturally composting as its racism becomes visible to a new generation. The new story (these giras) grows from the same soil: the same characters, the same magical farmstead, the same love of Brazilian folklore. What changes is the center of gravity. The hero isn't Pedrinho or Narizinho or even Emília. The hero is the Black grandmother who made them all possible and was written as a footnote in her own house.

items.name: The Sítio We're Writing — Family as Co-Authors

sections.About: The grammar's invitation: you are not reading a finished book. You are writing one. Pedro's family models what every family can do — take an inherited story (Lobato's Sítio, or any beloved-but-problematic text), open it in the presence of ancestors, and write something new in the margins. The gira is the method. The prescriptions are the practice. Emília is the bridge. The children are the continuation. This is Daniel Tiger to Mr. Rogers: the beloved framework translated for a generation that needs different medicine.
sections.For the Next Gira: This grammar is designed to be extended. The twelve giras here are a first cycle. Families who practice them will generate their own stories, their own prescriptions, their own encounters with the spirits. The book stays open. If your family writes a thirteenth gira — a conversation with Vovó Nastácia that these twelve didn't cover, a new entity who arrives, a prescription that the children invent — that IS the grammar working. You are not consumers of this content. You are the next authors. Vovó Nastácia is in the kitchen. She's waiting for you.
